notes: Cloud-level commitment discount tiers for v0.6.0. Conservative averages from each cloud's published RI / Savings Plan / CUD / Universal Credit calculators as of May 2026. Actual rates vary by instance family, region, payment option, and the cloud's quarterly pricing changes. Per-family tiers (e.g., compute-optimized vs general-purpose) are deferred to v0.6.x — these are deliberately conservative middle estimates; if a real workload returns numbers that look too low to justify a commitment, verify against the cloud's own calculator before signing.

aws._notes: Mix of EC2 Standard RIs + Compute Savings Plans + EC2 Instance Savings Plans across general-purpose / compute-optimized / memory-optimized families. Real rates: m5/t3 ~30-35% 1yr, ~52-62% 3yr; c5 ~32-38% 1yr, ~55-65% 3yr; r5 ~28-34% 1yr, ~50-58% 3yr.

azure._notes: Reserved VM Instances. Azure typically structures RIs as all-upfront with monthly billing option that has similar effective rate. 3yr discount averages slightly higher than AWS for D-series/E-series equivalents.

gcp._notes: Resource-based Committed Use Discounts (CUDs). GCP's CUDs are billed monthly without an upfront-payment option, so all upfront variants effectively map to the same rate as no-upfront. Spend-based CUDs can reach ~70% discount but are deferred from v0.6.0.

oci._notes: Annual Universal Credits / 3-year flex. OCI commitment discounts are smaller than the hyperscalers because OCI's on-demand pricing is already aggressive (especially with Always Free + 10 TB free egress). The break-even calculation often makes Universal Credits less compelling than just paying on-demand at OCI's already-low rates.
